name: Lulumahu Falls
tagline: A hidden 50-foot waterfall in the Nu'uanu Valley rainforest — muddy, jungle-choked, and worth every slippery step.
parking: Small pulloff on Pali Hwy near the Old Pali Road trailhead
bestTime: After rain for maximum flow; go early on weekdays to avoid crowds
talkStory: This 50-foot waterfall sits on the grounds of the former Nu'uanu reservoir system, and the trail crosses remnants of the old aqueduct infrastructure built in the early 1900s to supply Honolulu's water. The valley was sacred to ancient Hawaiians — lulu means 'calm/sheltered' and mahu means 'steam/mist,' describing the perpetual fog that blankets these falls. The trail weaves through dense bamboo groves and past massive banyan trees with aerial roots forming cathedral-like archways. Technically DLNR land requiring a permit — check current access rules before going.
